title: Working Tree, Index, Commits, and Atomic History · Conflict Triage
summary: Turn a mixed set of feature, formatting, and accidental changes into reviewable commits without losing work. Learners isolate plausible failures, compare competing hypotheses, and record cause-level repair evidence. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in an atomic commit construction workbook and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Trace HEAD, index, and working-tree versions of a path before using status, diff, add, restore, or commit.
Read branch, staged, unstaged, untracked, rename, conflict, and ahead/behind status without treating short output as context-free.
Select working-tree, staged, commit-to-commit, path-limited, word, and summary diffs from the review question.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# git-basics cumulative repository evidence notebook
# Git operations change only disposable simulated repository state.
# Inspect status, diffs, graph, refs, and recovery before every mutation.

steps.title: Inspect the failure record
steps.instruction:
debug case — README.md has one intended line, data/incidents.csv has an accidental edit, and evidence.txt is untracked. Construct one atomic commit by comparing working tree, index, and HEAD, then verify the staged diff. Competency probe: Trace HEAD, index, and working-tree versions of a path before using status, diff, add, restore, or commit.

Inspect the Three Tree Model trace for this debug case. Which record connects a precondition, observed state change, and boundary result?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for trace head, index, and working-tree versions of a path before using status, diff, add, restore, or commit.

steps.title: Implement the cause-level repair
steps.instruction: Append a safe repository investigation headed "# Evidence: git-atomic-commit-message-1tjr4m" with a concrete hypothesis, one bounded simulator command, and an observation that changes the next decision.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Atomic Commit Message into observable performance evidence.

# command: choose one bounded simulator observation
# observation: [replace with the result that changes the next decision]

content.title: Evidence is more than syntax
content.text: The Atomic Commit Message structural check is only one gate. an atomic commit construction workbook must also survive the stated changed case and causal explanation.

hints: Name the Atomic Commit Message precondition or invariant that the debug case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: README.md has one intended line, data/incidents.csv has an accidental edit, and evidence.txt is untracked. Construct one atomic commit by comparing working tree, index, and HEAD, then verify the staged diff. Competency probe: Create a cohesive commit with an imperative summary, rationale, test evidence, and no accidental generated or secret data.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: A large commit is acceptable when its message lists every changed file.

steps.title: Publish the incident record
steps.instruction: Write the debug evidence note for Atomic Commit Message: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — README.md has one intended line, data/incidents.csv has an accidental edit, and evidence.txt is untracked. Construct one atomic commit by comparing working tree, index, and HEAD, then verify the staged diff. Competency probe: Create a cohesive commit with an imperative summary, rationale, test evidence, and no accidental generated or secret data.
steps.why: Retrieving Atomic Commit Message through the concrete an atomic commit construction workbook case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Create a cohesive commit with an imperative summary, rationale, test evidence, and no accidental generated or secret data.
